subject: Chemistry
q: Equal masses of methane and oxygen are mixed in an empty container at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The fraction of the total pressure exerted by oxygen is
question: Equal masses of methane and oxygen are mixed in an empty container at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The fraction of the total pressure exerted by oxygen is
solution:
Let the mass of methane and oxygen is $w$
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { mole fraction of oxygen }=\frac{\frac{w}{32}}{\frac{w}{32}+\frac{w}{16}} \\
& =\frac{\frac{1}{32}}{\frac{1}{32}+\frac{1}{16}}=\frac{\frac{1}{32}}{\frac{3}{32}}=\frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}$
Let the total pressure be $\mathrm{P}$
The pressure exerted by oxygen (partial pressure) $=\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{O}_2} \times \mathrm{P}_{\text {total }}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{P} \times \frac{1}{3}$
Hence, (C) is correct.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Activated charcoal adsorbs SO2 more efficiently than CH4.

Reason R : Gases with lower critical temperatures are readily adsorbed by activated charcoal. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Activated charcoal adsorbs SO2 more efficiently than CH4.

Reason R : Gases with lower critical temperatures are readily adsorbed by activated charcoal. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> are correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is not correct.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is not correct but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is correct.
solution:
Lesser the value of critical temperature of gases lesser will be the extent of adsorption.

Gas SO2 CH4
Critical temperature/K 630 190

The critical temperature depends on the strength of the intermolecular interactions that hold a substance together as a liquid.

SO2 has stronger intermolecular force of attractions than CH4, due to the more molar mass and non-zero dipole moment.

So, Activated charcoal adsorbs SO2 more efficiently than CH4. is correct statement but Reason statement is not correct.

question: Sulphur dioxide and oxygen were allowed​ to diffuse through a porous partition. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>dm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> diffuses through the porous partition in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn></math> seconds. The volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>dm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> which diffuses under the similar condition in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> seconds will be (atomic mass of sulphur<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>32</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>);
solution:
As per Graham's law,

V SO 2 / t SO 2 V O 2 / t O 2 = p SO 2 p O 2 M O 2 M SO 2 = M O 2 M SO 2

2 0 dm 3 / 6 0 sec V O 2 / 3 0 sec = 3 2 6 4 = 1 1 · 4 1 4

2 0 × 3 0 6 0 × V O 2 = 1 1 · 4 1 4 ⇒ V O 2 = 1 0 × 1 · 4 1 4

= 14.14 dm3

nCH4=PVRT

=1×4×103×10000.083×300

Weight of CH4

=40×16×1050.083×300gm

=25.7×105 gm

subject: Chemistry
q: For $1 \mathrm{~mol}$ of an ideal gas at a constant temperature $T$, the plot of $(\log P)$ against $(\log V)$ is a ( $P$ : Pressure, $V$ : Volume)
question: For $1 \mathrm{~mol}$ of an ideal gas at a constant temperature $T$, the plot of $(\log P)$ against $(\log V)$ is a ( $P$ : Pressure, $V$ : Volume)
options: Straight line with a negative slope.
solution:
According to Boyle's law, $P V=$ constant
$\therefore \log P+\log V=$ constant
$\log P=-\log V+$ constant
Hence, the plot of $\log P$ vs $\log V$ is straight line with negative slope.

subject: Chemistry
q:
A box contains 0.90 g of liquid water in equilibrium with water vapour at 27°C. The equilibrium vapour pressure of water at 27°C 32.0 Torr. When the volume of the box is increased, some of the liquid water evaporates to maintain the equilibrium pressure. If all the liquid water evaporates, then the volume of the box must be ______ litre. [nearest integer]

(Given: R=0.082 L atm K-1 mol-1_

(Ignore the volume of the liquid water and assume water vapours behave as an ideal gas.)
question:
A box contains 0.90 g of liquid water in equilibrium with water vapour at 27°C. The equilibrium vapour pressure of water at 27°C 32.0 Torr. When the volume of the box is increased, some of the liquid water evaporates to maintain the equilibrium pressure. If all the liquid water evaporates, then the volume of the box must be ______ litre. [nearest integer]

(Given: R=0.082 L atm K-1 mol-1_

(Ignore the volume of the liquid water and assume water vapours behave as an ideal gas.)
solution:
Applying ideal gas equation,PV=nRTwhere, P= pressureV=volumen=Number of molesR=Universal gas constantT=TemperatureV=nRTP=0.90×0.82×300×76018×32=29.21Litres

Hence, the volume of the box is 29 litres(In integer)

question: The temperature at which oxygen molecules have the same root mean square speed as helium atoms have at 300 K is : (Atomic masses : He = 4 u, O = 16 u)
solution:
C rms = 3 RT M

C rms He = 3 R × 3 0 0 4 & C rms O 2 = 3 R × T O 2 3 2

∴   ( C rms ) He = ( C rms ) O 2 ⇒   3R×300 4 = 3R× T O 2 32

T O 2 = 3 0 0 × 3 2 4 = 2 4 0 0 K

q:
Consider the van der Waal's constants, a and b, for the following gases.
Gas Ar Ne Kr Xe
a/(atm dm6 mol-2) 1.3 0.2 5.1 4.1
b/(10-2 dm3 mol-1) 3.2 1.7 1.0 5.0

Which gas is expected to have the highest critical temperature?
question:
Consider the van der Waal's constants, a and b, for the following gases.
Gas Ar Ne Kr Xe
a/(atm dm6 mol-2) 1.3 0.2 5.1 4.1
b/(10-2 dm3 mol-1) 3.2 1.7 1.0 5.0

Which gas is expected to have the highest critical temperature?
solution:
Critical temperature is :-

TC=8a27Rb
for Kr the value of ab is the highest so, Tc for Kr will be highest.
ab for Ar=1.33.2=0.406
ab for Ne=0.21.7=0.118
ab for Kr=5.11=5.1
ab for Xe=4.15=0.82

subject: Chemistry
q:
The pressure exerted by a non-reactive gaseous mixture of 6.4 g of methane and 8.8 g of carbon dioxide in a 10 L vessel at 27°C is _____kPa. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).

[Assume gases are ideal, R=8.314 J mol-1 K-1 Atomic masses: C :12.0 u, H :1.0 u,  O :16.0 u]
question:
The pressure exerted by a non-reactive gaseous mixture of 6.4 g of methane and 8.8 g of carbon dioxide in a 10 L vessel at 27°C is _____kPa. (Round off to the Nearest Integer).

[Assume gases are ideal, R=8.314 J mol-1 K-1 Atomic masses: C :12.0 u, H :1.0 u,  O :16.0 u]
solution:
Total moles of gases, n=nCH4+nCO2

=6.416+8.844=0.6

Now, P=nRTV=0.6×8.314×30010×10-3

=1.49652×105 Pa=149.652 kPa

≈150 kPa

(Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Assume chlorine is an ideal gas at STP <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>083</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo> </mo><mi>bar</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>023</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><mo>]</mo></math>
question: The number of chlorine atoms in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of chlorine gas at STP is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>21</mn></msup></math>. (Round off to the Nearest Integer). [Assume chlorine is an ideal gas at STP <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>083</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo> </mo><mi>bar</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>,</mo><msub><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>023</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><mo>]</mo></math>
solution:
PV=nRT

1.0×201000=N6.023×1023×0.083×273

∴ Number of Cl2 molecules, N=5.3×1020

Hence, Number of Cl -atoms =1.06×1021

≈1×1021

subject: Chemistry
q: A certain quantity of real gas occupies a volume of 0.15 dm3 at 100 atm and 500 K when its compressibility factor is 1.07. Its volume at 300 atm and 300 K (When its compressibility factor is 1.4) is ______×10-4dm3(Nearest integer)
question: A certain quantity of real gas occupies a volume of 0.15 dm3 at 100 atm and 500 K when its compressibility factor is 1.07. Its volume at 300 atm and 300 K (When its compressibility factor is 1.4) is ______×10-4dm3(Nearest integer)
solution:
The compressibility factor (Z) is given as:

Z = PVnRT

n=PVZRT

Z1=1.07, P1=100atm, V1=0.15L, T1=500KZ2=1.4, P1=300atm, V2=?, T2=300K

Z1Z2=P1V1×T2T1×P2V2

1.071.4=100×0.15×300500×300×V2

V2=0.03925 dm3

subject: Chemistry
q:
A 10 g mixture of hydrogen and helium is contained in a vessel of capacity 0.0125 m3 at 6 bar and 27 °C. The mass of helium in the mixture is ..... g.

(Given : R=8.3JK-1 mol-1 (Atomic masses of H and He are 1u and 4u, respectively)
question:
A 10 g mixture of hydrogen and helium is contained in a vessel of capacity 0.0125 m3 at 6 bar and 27 °C. The mass of helium in the mixture is ..... g.

(Given : R=8.3JK-1 mol-1 (Atomic masses of H and He are 1u and 4u, respectively)
solution:
PV=nmixRT

nmix=6×12.50.083×300≈3

Out of total 3 moles Let mole of He=x

Mole of H2=3-x

Then total mass of the mixture will be = 4x+23-x=10

Number of moles of He gas x=2 mol

Mass of He= number of moles × molar mass = 8 g

q:
Geraniol, a volatile organic compound, is a component of rose oil. The density of the vapour is 0.46gL-1 at 257 °C and 100 mmHg. The molar mass of geraniol is (Nearest Integer)
[Given R=0.082 L atm K-1mol-1]
question:
Geraniol, a volatile organic compound, is a component of rose oil. The density of the vapour is 0.46gL-1 at 257 °C and 100 mmHg. The molar mass of geraniol is (Nearest Integer)
[Given R=0.082 L atm K-1mol-1]
solution:
Assuming ideal behaviour P=dRTM

P=100760 atm, T=257+273=530 K

d=0.46gm/L

So M=0.46×0.082×530100×760

=151.93≈152

subject: Chemistry
q:
Two flasks I and II shown below are connected by a valve of negligible volume.

When the valve is opened, the final pressure of the system in bar is x×10-2. The value of x is (Integer answer)
[Assume : Ideal gas; 1 bar =105 Pa : Molar mass of N2=28.0 mol-1;R=8.31 J mol-1 K-1
question:
Two flasks I and II shown below are connected by a valve of negligible volume.

When the valve is opened, the final pressure of the system in bar is x×10-2. The value of x is (Integer answer)
[Assume : Ideal gas; 1 bar =105 Pa : Molar mass of N2=28.0 mol-1;R=8.31 J mol-1 K-1
solution:
Applying ; n-I+n-IIinitial =The final number of moles.

n-I and n-II are the number of moles of gas in each container.

⇒ Assuming the system attains a final temperature of T (such that 300<T<60)

⇒∣ Heat lost by N2 of container (I)|=| Heat gained by N2 of container II|}

⇒n-ICm300-T)=n-IICm(T-60)

⇒2.828(300-T)=0.228( T-60)

⇒14(300-T)=T-60

⇒(14×300+60)15=T

⇒T=284 K (final temperature)

⇒ If the final pressure =P

⇒nfinal =3.028

⇒PRTVI+VII=3.0gm28gm/mol

P=328 mol×8.31Jmol-K×284 K3×10-3 m3×105barPa

⇒0.84287bar

⇒84.28×10-2 bar 

⇒84

subject: Chemistry
q: By how many folds the temperature of a gas would increase when the root mean square velocity of the gas molecules in a container of fixed volume is increased from $5 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$ to $10 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$ ?
question: By how many folds the temperature of a gas would increase when the root mean square velocity of the gas molecules in a container of fixed volume is increased from $5 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$ to $10 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$ ?
solution:
r.m.s. velocity $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}$
i.e., $\frac{\mathrm{V}_1}{\mathrm{~V}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}}$
$\frac{5 \times 10^4}{10 \times 10^4}=\frac{1}{2}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}}$
$\therefore \mathrm{T}_2=4 \mathrm{T}_1$

subject: Chemistry
q:
The pressure of a moist gas at 27°C is 4 atm. The volume of the container is doubled at the same temperature. The new pressure of the moist gas is …×10-1 atm. (Nearest integer)
(Given: The vapour pressure of water at 27°C is 0.4 atm)
question:
The pressure of a moist gas at 27°C is 4 atm. The volume of the container is doubled at the same temperature. The new pressure of the moist gas is …×10-1 atm. (Nearest integer)
(Given: The vapour pressure of water at 27°C is 0.4 atm)
solution:
Dalton's Law states that the total pressure P of moist air is the sum of partial pressures of dry air Pa and water vapor Pv: P= Pa+Pv. Only the absolute pressure is the state parameter of moist air.

Pgas+PH2O=4

Pgas=4-0.4=3.6 atm

As volume is doubled, Pgasnew =1.8 atm

New Total Pressure =1.8+0.4=2.2 atm

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following statements is NOT true about the effect of an increase in temperature on the distribution of molecular speeds in a gas?
question: Which one of the following statements is NOT true about the effect of an increase in temperature on the distribution of molecular speeds in a gas?
options: The fraction of the molecules with the most probable speed increases
The area under the distribution curve remains the same as under the lower temperature
solution: Most probable velocity increase and fraction of molecule possessing most probable velocity decreases

subject: Chemistry
q: At constant temperature, a gas is at a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>940</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Hg</mi></math>. The pressure at which its volume decreases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>%</mo></math> is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Hg</mi></math>. (Nearest integer)
question: At constant temperature, a gas is at a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>940</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Hg</mi></math>. The pressure at which its volume decreases by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>%</mo></math> is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Hg</mi></math>. (Nearest integer)
solution:
From Boyle's Law,

P2V2=P1V1 

P1 =940.3 mm of Hg

P2 = Pressure at which volume is reduced by 40%

V2 = Volume decreased by 40%

 V2=V1-0.4 V1=0.6 V1

Then,P2=940.3×V10.6V1

=1567.16 

≈1567 mm Hg

subject: Chemistry
q: for a real gas at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> temperature and high pressure (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>99</mn></math> bar) the value of compressibility factor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>, so the value of Van 1der Waal's constant '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math>' should be_____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>083</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> bar <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: for a real gas at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> temperature and high pressure (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>99</mn></math> bar) the value of compressibility factor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>, so the value of Van 1der Waal's constant '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math>' should be_____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> (Given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>083</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> bar <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
For real gas under high pressure we can neglect pressure corrections due to intermolecular forces. But since volume will be low, we can not neglect volume corrections due to molecular size.

Z=1+PbRT = 2 ⇒b=RTP

=0.083×29899

=0.25×10-2 L mol-1

subject: Chemistry
q:
The number of statement's, which are correct with respect to the compression of carbon dioxide from point (a) in the Andrews isotherm from the following is _________.

A. Carbon dioxide remains as a gas upto point (b)
B. Liquid carbon dioxide appears at point (c)
C. Liquid and gaseous carbon dioxide coexist between points (b) and (c)
D. As the volume decreases from (b) to (c), the amount of liquid decreases
question:
The number of statement's, which are correct with respect to the compression of carbon dioxide from point (a) in the Andrews isotherm from the following is _________.

A. Carbon dioxide remains as a gas upto point (b)
B. Liquid carbon dioxide appears at point (c)
C. Liquid and gaseous carbon dioxide coexist between points (b) and (c)
D. As the volume decreases from (b) to (c), the amount of liquid decreases
solution:
Andrews noticed that at high temperatures isotherms look like that of an ideal gas and the gas cannot be liquefied even at very high pressure. As the temperature is lowered, shape of the curve changes and data shows considerable deviation from ideal behaviour.

Carbon dioxide remains as a gas only up to point b. 

At point b, liquid of a particular volume appears. Further compression does not change the pressure. Liquid and gaseous carbon dioxide coexist and further application of pressure results in the condensation of more gas until the point c is reached. At point c, all the gas has been condensed and further application of pressure merely compresses the liquid as shown by steep line. A point like d represents liquid state

q: The intermolecular interaction that is dependent on the inverse cube of distance between the molecules is:
question: The intermolecular interaction that is dependent on the inverse cube of distance between the molecules is:
solution:
For Hydrogen bond which is a case of dipole-dipole interaction, intermolecular force

F∝1r3

r is distance between two dipoles
